title: 1. Asset Valuations - Financial Stability Report - April 2025
summary: The Federal Reserve's April 2025 Financial Stability Report chapter on Asset Valuations found that prices remained elevated relative to fundamentals across most asset classes despite a 6+ percent equity decline and heightened volatility in early April following trade policy announcements. Treasury market liquidity stayed low by historical standards with considerable yield volatility, though trading remained orderly. The report provides detailed size and growth data for major asset markets including equities ($70.3 trillion), residential real estate ($59.7 trillion), and Treasury securities ($28.1 trillion).
key_points: Treasury market liquidity was low by historical standards heading into April, with yields exhibiting considerable volatility that further deteriorated liquidity
Trading remained orderly in early April despite volatility spikes, with markets functioning without serious disruption
Interest rate volatility implied by interest rate swaps was elevated by historical standards and increased further in early April
Equity prices fell more than 6 percent from the previous Financial Stability Report through April 11, but remained high relative to forecasted earnings
Corporate bond spreads widened significantly but stayed at or below historical medians; issuance slowed considerably
The nominal Treasury term premium fell slightly but remained near the top of its range since 2010, near its longer-term historical median
The price-to-earnings ratio (price to expected 12-month earnings) remained well above its historical median through March
Residential real estate prices relative to fundamentals continued to be well above historical averages despite CRE showing some stabilizing signs
why_it_matters: This Financial Stability Report section provides systemic risk context for market participants, policymakers, and researchers. The assessment of elevated asset valuations combined with low Treasury market liquidity and heightened volatility informs understanding of potential vulnerabilities in the financial system. The data on equity markets trading at stretched valuations while experiencing significant drawdowns is relevant for risk management and portfolio construction decisions.
macro_context: The report attributes early April market volatility to U.S. trade policy announcements, which created heightened uncertainty about global trade patterns, increased recession risk perceptions, and inflation concerns. This provides context for the macro drivers of market moves during the period.
market_structure_context: Treasury market liquidity remained low by historical standards entering April. Early April saw considerable Treasury yield volatility that further deteriorated market liquidity. Interest rate volatility (implied vol from interest rate swaps) was elevated by historical standards and increased further in early April. Despite volatility, trading remained orderly and markets functioned without serious disruption. The Treasury yield curve steepened as shorter-maturity yields fell.
important_numbers: $59,656 billion — total residential real estate value
−2.9% — commercial real estate price growth (real, 1-year through Dec 2024)
6%+ — equity price decline from previous FSR through April 11
notes: Historical record from April 2025 Federal Reserve Financial Stability Report. Relevant to market structure (Treasury liquidity, volatility metrics, term premium) and macro catalysts (trade policy as driver of early April volatility). Source body truncated but core market structure context preserved. Confidence slightly lowered due to age and truncation.
